question.text: A company has an average operating profit of Rs. 20 crore over the past 3 years, but incurred a loss in one of those years. Is it eligible for an IPO under Regulation 6(1)?

answer.text: Yes, because the average exceeds Rs. 15 crore
No, because operating profit must be positive in each of the 3 years, not just on average
Yes, if the loss year was the earliest of the three years
No, because the average operating profit must be at least Rs. 25 crore

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Regulation 6(1) requires both conditions to be met: (a) average operating profit of at least Rs. 15 crore over 3 years, AND (b) the company must have earned an operating profit in each of the 3 preceding years. A loss in any single year disqualifies the company, regardless of how high the average may be. **The Trap:** The average exceeding Rs. 15 crore may seem sufficient, but the "profit in each year" requirement is a separate, conjunctive condition. This is a classic exam trap that tests whether candidates read both parts of the regulation. **Cross-Exam Context:** This type of conjunctive regulatory condition — where ALL criteria must be met simultaneously — is a pattern seen across NISM certifications. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When evaluating IPO readiness, do not rely on averages alone. Review each year's financials individually. If one year shows a loss, the company must either wait or use the alternative QIB route (book building with 75% QIB allotment).
